oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Hybrid/Pied Mutation Macaw
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Aviculture, American Federation of
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 49 Number 3 (April 2022).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.In recent years there has been a resurgence in interest in hybrid macaws for many reasons ranging from the listing of so many species of macaws on the Endangered Species List which restricts their sales and transport to a preference for some of the most spectacularly colored birds anywhere. Some of the breeding has been planned and some is the result of accidental breeding by pets who have lived together for years. Where hybrids are concerned, there is no guarantee as to what the progeny will look like and occasionally there are some very interesting surprises.Below is a hybrid and possible mutation that was bred by Dr. Susan Clubb. The baby is an F2 Catalina macaw which means that both parents are also hybrids and in this case both parents are a cross between a scarlet macaw and a blue and gold macaw which makes them Catalinas. Dr. Clubb believes that this offspring is a spontaneous pied mutation and she is not aware of other pied macaws. (Check out the baby’s white spotted feet.) His siblings have all looked like Catalinas but while some have had more brick red on the chest or more yellow on the head and face, none have had the white feet with black dots or yellow at the ends of the wings.Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 49 No. 3 (2022): Watchbird April 2022; 25
